turns.content:
<gray_area>Dead code deletion order: 8 dead files found across src/ — schema-kernel/archive (toggle-gates.ts, permission.schema.ts, tool-definition.schema.ts), coordination/recovery/ (5 files, 763 LOC), features/runtime-detection/ (195 LOC), features/steering-engine/ (609 LOC). Should deletions be one-by-one per atomic commit, batch per module, or all in one pass?</gray_area>
<phase_context>Phase 18: Root cleanup, sync boundary, sync manifest. Execute on Phase 17 findings. Depends on Phase 17 (discovery audit complete).</phase_context>
<project_context>Hivemind — Runtime Composition Engine for OpenCode. TypeScript strict, Node.js >= 20, 125 test files, 1767 tests, 16 custom tools.</project_context>
<calibration_tier>standard</calibration_tier>

Research this gray area and return a structured comparison table with rationale covering: Option | Pros | Cons | Complexity | Recommendation.

Here is the research result for this gray area.

**Note from dependency audit:** The gray area description misidentifies some files. `src/schema-kernel/permission.schema.ts` and `tool-definition.schema.ts` are actively exported via the barrel and consumed by `cross-primitive-validator.ts`/`primitive-loader.ts` — they are **not** dead code. `src/coordination/recovery/` and `src/features/runtime-detection/` directories already don't exist. The actual dead code totals **6 files (795 LOC)**: toggle-gates.ts + companion test + 3 steering-engine files. Steering-engine has internal cross-imports (types.ts, steering-state.ts → steering-policy.schema.ts) forcing atomic module-level deletion.

---

## Dead code deletion order

| Option | Pros | Cons | Complexity | Recommendation |
|--------|------|------|------------|----------------|
| **All-in-one pass** (1 commit, 6 files) | Single clean commit with descriptive message, easiest to revert as a unit, zero intermediate broken states, trivial code review | Poor git bisect granularity (can't attribute regression to a specific file), larger single diff | 6 files across 3 dirs (`hooks/transforms/`, `features/steering-engine/`, `tests/hooks/`) — Risk: none (pure deletions, zero behavioral change) | Rec if bisect resolution is not critical and team prefers clean history |
| **Module-batched** (2 commits — toggle-gates pair then steering-engine trio) | Groups by module boundary for sensible review, each commit self-contained and reversible, intermediate state never broken | Midpoint (commit 1 after toggle-gates) leaves dead steering-engine still present | Commit 1: 2 files (toggle-gates.ts + test) — Risk: none. Commit 2: 3 files (steering-engine trio) — Risk: none | **Rec if module accountability matters** — PR review per logical unit is the standard approach for this project |
| **Per-file atomic** (5 sequential commits) | Maximum git bisect precision, each file individually revertible, fine-grained blame trail | Steering-engine internal deps (types.ts → steering-policy.schema.ts) force ordering — intermediate commits WILL produce broken builds if ordered wrong; 5× commit overhead | 5 files sequentially, but steering-engine trio cannot be split without compilation breakage — effectively forces 5 commits where 2 must be grouped, defeating the purpose | Rec only if git bisect on file-deletion timing is critical (unlikely for dead code) |

**Rationale:** The dependency audit reveals that `steering-engine`'s internal import chain (types.ts + steering-state.ts both import `steering-policy.schema.ts`) forces atomic module-level deletion — all 3 files must go in a single commit to avoid intermediate compilation failure. Similarly, `toggle-gates.ts` and its companion test must be deleted together. This leaves two genuinely viable strategies. **Module-batched** (2 commits) is the recommended approach for this project: one commit deleting the toggle-gates pair (`hooks/transforms/toggle-gates.ts` + `tests/hooks/toggle-gates.test.ts`), a second committing the steering-engine trio. This keeps coherent module boundaries, avoids intermediate broken states, and produces reviewable per-module diffs. All-in-one is acceptable if the orchestrator prioritizes speed over module accountability, given the deletion-only scope carries zero behavioral risk. Per-file atomic is the weakest option because steering-engine's internal deps defeat its primary advantage (clean per-file bisect).
